name: Los Lunas Stone
description: The Los Lunas Stone is a large boulder located near Los Lunas, New Mexico, bearing an inscription that some researchers claim is an abbreviated version of the Ten Commandments written in Paleo-Hebrew or a related alphabet. Proponents of its authenticity argue that the inscription provides evidence of pre-Columbian contact between Semitic peoples and the Americas, suggesting a transoceanic migration far earlier than commonly accepted. The stone was reportedly discovered in the 1930s or earlier, and its existence was publicized in the 1980s. The inscription consists of symbols that bear resemblance to ancient Hebrew letters, leading some to believe it is a shortened form of the Decalogue. However, mainstream archaeologists and linguists generally consider the inscription to be a modern hoax or a relatively recent petroglyph created by someone with a rudimentary knowledge of Hebrew or a similar script. They point to inconsistencies in the letter forms, the geological context of the inscription, and the lack of corroborating archaeological evidence in the area. The absence of a clear archaeological context and the possibility of modern manipulation cast serious doubt on its authenticity as evidence of ancient transoceanic contact. The weathered appearance of the stone is often cited by proponents as evidence of its age, but weathering can occur relatively quickly in desert environments.
subtitle: Ancient Hebrew inscription in New Mexico?

claims.claim: The inscription is Paleo-Hebrew and predates Columbus.
claims.evidence: Similarity of the symbols to Paleo-Hebrew letters; weathering of the stone.
claims.criticism: Letter forms are inconsistent with known Paleo-Hebrew alphabets; weathering is not a reliable indicator of age; lack of archaeological context.

claims.claim: The inscription is an abbreviated version of the Ten Commandments.
claims.evidence: Recognition of recognizable Hebrew words relating to the Ten Commandments.
claims.criticism: The inscription is not a complete or accurate representation of the Ten Commandments; some symbols are not readily identifiable as Hebrew letters.

claims.claim: The stone provides evidence of pre-Columbian Semitic contact with the Americas.
claims.evidence: The presence of Hebrew-like writing suggests ancient Semitic travelers.
claims.criticism: No other archaeological evidence supports pre-Columbian Semitic contact in the area; the inscription is likely a modern creation.

keyFigures.name: David Deal
keyFigures.role: Early proponent and researcher of the stone.

keyFigures.name: George E. Morehouse
keyFigures.role: Reported the stone's existence publicly in the 1980s.

keyFigures.name: Frank Hibben
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who reportedly knew about the stone in the 1930s, though his involvement is debated.

keyFigures.name: Kenneth Feder
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist and skeptic who has critiqued the claims about the stone.

mythologyConnections: Lost Tribes of Israel (indirectly, as some proponents link pre-Columbian contact to their dispersal)
Garden of Eden theories in America (tangentially, as some extreme theories tie ancient migrations to biblical locations)

timeline.event: Reported discovery of the Los Lunas Stone.
Publicity of the Los Lunas Stone by George E. Morehouse and others.
Continued debate and research regarding the stone's authenticity and origin.

sources.author: Fell, Barry
sources.work: America B.C.: Ancient Settlers in the New World
